metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure consult for POEM (achalasia type II, failed dilation, nutrition decline)

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient takes nifedipine, bites a capsule sublingually before meals to help swallowing, prescribed by prior GI
planted_discrepancies.note: active CCB not on chart; hypotension/anesthesia relevance for POEM and must be reconciled

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient's 'blood thinner' is an over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3) supplement, not a prescription anticoagulant
planted_discrepancies.note: hedged term; not a true anticoagulant but fish oil should be documented and held pre-POEM per bleeding protocol — do not clear as if on warfarin/DOAC

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient reports ~20 lb loss over ~3 months and still declining, now on mostly liquids
planted_discrepancies.note: nutrition decline understated in referral; reinforces expedite

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient also had a botulinum toxin injection to the LES about 2 years ago at an outside hospital
planted_discrepancies.note: prior botox causes submucosal fibrosis and raises POEM technical difficulty; not captured in chart

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: penicillin caused a rash in childhood
patient takes OTC omeprazole 20 mg most days for heartburn
planted_discrepancies.note: OTC PPI not captured; relevant to post-POEM reflux baseline

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: Achalasia type II confirmed on high-resolution manometry (Chicago classification)
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms the pressure/swallow study and the diagnosis

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: prior pneumatic dilation x1, did not durably help
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms one balloon dilation that helped only briefly

prior_notes.text: New patient consult for progressive dysphagia to solids and liquids over ~9 months with regurgitation of undigested food and nocturnal cough. Barium esophagram showed dilated esophagus with a 'bird-beak' tapering at the gastroesophageal junction and delayed emptying. High-resolution esophageal manometry: elevated integrated relaxation pressure with 100% failed peristalsis and pan-esophageal pressurization — consistent with achalasia, Chicago classification type II. EGD ruled out mechanical obstruction/pseudoachalasia. Weight down ~8 lb since symptom onset. Discussed treatment options including pneumatic dilation, POEM, and Heller myotomy. Plan: trial pneumatic dilation; refer for POEM evaluation if inadequate response.

Endoscopic pneumatic (balloon) dilation of the lower esophageal sphincter, 30 mm, for achalasia type II. Tolerated procedure; no immediate perforation on post-dilation esophagram. Patient reported transient improvement in swallowing for ~2 weeks followed by recurrence of dysphagia and regurgitation. Given failure of dilation and ongoing nutritional decline, refer for peroral endoscopic myotomy (POEM).

code.text: Achalasia, Chicago classification type II (confirmed on high-resolution manometry)

coding.display: Blood pressure panel with all children optional

code.text: Endoscopic pneumatic (balloon) dilation of LES, 30 mm

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Elevated median integrated relaxation pressure (IRP 22 mmHg), 100% failed peristalsis with pan-esophageal pressurization in ≥20% of swallows. Consistent with achalasia, Chicago classification type II.
Dilated esophagus with smooth 'bird-beak' tapering at the gastroesophageal junction and delayed barium emptying. Findings compatible with achalasia.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Cho — thanks for coming in. This is the visit where we go over the referral for the POEM, the procedure to loosen the muscle at the bottom of your esophagus, and I ask you a lot of questions. How are things?
MA: Before you get started, doctor — I roomed Ms. Cho and got her vitals: height 5 foot 6, weight 114 pounds, temp 36.8, blood pressure 118 over 74, heart rate 76, respirations 14, oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Honestly not great. Swallowing has gotten worse. I'm basically on soups and smoothies now — anything solid comes right back up.
DR: I'm sorry. The referral mentions about twelve pounds down. Does that sound right?
PT: It's more than that now. I've lost around twenty pounds since the spring, and it's still going. My clothes are hanging off me.
DR: Okay, that's important — we'll flag the nutrition side. Let me go through your medications. The chart actually lists nothing. Is that right, you take nothing?
PT: Well — I do bite one of those nifedipine capsules under my tongue before meals. A previous GI doctor gave them to me to help the food go down. And I take omeprazole from the drugstore most days for heartburn.
DR: That's really useful — neither of those is in our record, so I'll add them. Do you take any blood thinner?
PT: Yes, a blood thinner — well, fish oil. The omega-3 capsules. My sister said they thin your blood.
DR: Ah, that's a good thing to sort out. Fish oil is a supplement, not a prescription blood thinner — you're not on warfarin or anything like that?
PT: No, nothing like that. Just the fish oil.
DR: Got it. We'll still have you hold the fish oil before the procedure. Now the referral says you had one dilation that didn't hold. Anything else done before that?
PT: Yeah — a couple years ago at another hospital they did an injection, botox I think, into the muscle. It helped for a month, maybe.
DR: Thank you — that matters for how we do the POEM. And allergies? Nothing's listed.
PT: Penicillin gave me a rash as a kid.
DR: I'll add that. So: we'll expedite the POEM for the weight loss, do the pre-op liquid diet and a screen per protocol, and reconcile all this. The team will call with prep details.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Cho is a 36-year-old woman presenting for pre-procedure consultation for planned peroral endoscopic myotomy (POEM). She has achalasia, Chicago classification type II, confirmed on high-resolution manometry (2026-02), with a barium esophagram showing bird-beak tapering and delayed emptying. She underwent one endoscopic pneumatic dilation (30 mm, 2026-04) with only ~2 weeks of relief. She now reports worsening dysphagia to solids and liquids, tolerating mostly liquids, with regurgitation of undigested food. She reports ~20 lb weight loss over the past ~3 months that is ongoing — greater than the 12 lb documented in the referral.

Medication reconciliation revealed several items absent from the chart: she takes nifedipine, biting a capsule sublingually before meals (prescribed by a prior GI for dysphagia), and OTC omeprazole 20 mg most days for heartburn. When asked about a blood thinner she reported taking a 'blood thinner,' which on clarification is an over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3) supplement, not a prescription anticoagulant. She additionally reports a prior botulinum toxin injection to the LES ~2 years ago at an outside facility, not previously documented. She reports a penicillin allergy (childhood rash), not previously on file.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 76, RR 14, BP 118/74, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 168 cm (5'6"), Wt 51.9 kg (114 lb), BMI 18.4. Labs — albumin 3.3 g/dL (2026-07-10), hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL. Manometry: IRP 22 mmHg, 100% failed peristalsis with pan-esophageal pressurization (type II). Esophagram: bird-beak GEJ with delayed emptying.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Achalasia type II, refractory to pneumatic dilation, with ongoing nutritional decline — appropriate POEM candidate; expedite given weight loss and low BMI/albumin.
- Nutrition: ~20 lb loss (understated in referral), BMI 18.4, albumin 3.3 — nutrition referral; monitor.
- Pre-op protocol: extended clear-liquid diet 48 h prior (esophageal food stasis); Candida esophagitis screen per POEM protocol.
- Medication reconciliation: ADD nifedipine (sublingual pre-meal) — hold/coordinate with anesthesia given hypotension risk under sedation; ADD OTC omeprazole 20 mg daily; document fish oil (omega-3) supplement and HOLD prior to POEM per bleeding protocol. Patient is NOT on a prescription anticoagulant.
- Document prior LES botulinum toxin injection (~2 yr ago) — anticipate submucosal fibrosis and increased POEM technical difficulty; counsel and plan accordingly.
- Add allergy: penicillin (childhood rash) — relevant to peri-procedure antibiotics.
- Anesthesia clearance and POEM scheduling; team to provide prep instructions and return precautions.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your planned POEM procedure for achalasia
• Worsening swallowing and continued weight loss
• Your medicines and supplements
• Getting your record corrected before the procedure

Next steps
• We will schedule your POEM and send prep instructions, including a clear-liquid diet for 48 hours before.
• We are expediting the procedure because of your weight loss; we placed a nutrition referral.
• We updated your record: added nifedipine (that you use before meals) and your over-the-counter omeprazole, noted your fish oil supplement to stop before the procedure, added your penicillin allergy, and noted your earlier botox injection.
• Your 'blood thinner' is fish oil, not a prescription blood thinner — please hold it before the procedure as we will instruct.
• Anesthesia will review your case; the team will call with timing and what to stop and when.
• Call for chest pain, fever, vomiting blood, or inability to keep down liquids.
